questions.question:
Write a letter to your Local Government Chairman complaining about the poor state of roads in your area and requesting urgent repair.
(Your address, date, salutation, body with at least 3 paragraphs, and appropriate closing are required.)
questions.answer:
12 Murtala Mohammed Street,
Ilorin South,
Kwara State.
15th March 2024.

The Chairman,
Ilorin South Local Government Area,
Ilorin,
Kwara State.

Dear Sir,

LETTER OF COMPLAINT ABOUT THE DEPLORABLE STATE OF ROADS IN ILORIN SOUTH

I write on behalf of the residents of Murtala Mohammed Street and its environs to draw your urgent attention to the extremely poor condition of roads in our area, which has become a source of great distress to our community.

The roads in our neighbourhood have deteriorated significantly over the past two years. Deep potholes now cover most of the road surfaces, making them virtually impassable during the rainy season. Vehicles are frequently damaged, motorcycles have had fatal accidents, and pedestrians — especially school children and the elderly — are constantly at risk of injury. Several businesses in the area have also suffered losses as delivery trucks avoid our streets entirely.

Despite repeated verbal complaints to your office, no action has been taken to address this problem. We understand that the local government has responsibilities and limited resources, but we respectfully submit that the safety and wellbeing of residents must be a priority. We therefore urgently request that your administration immediately commence grading and patching of the affected roads, and include full reconstruction of the worst sections in the next budget cycle.

We are confident that your administration will treat this matter with the urgency it deserves. We look forward to your prompt response and action.

Yours faithfully,

Adeola Bello
(Chairman, Residents' Association)

questions.question:
Write a composition on the topic: 'The Importance of Education in National Development' (approximately 450 words).

Your essay should have:
(a) An introduction
(b) At least three developed body paragraphs
(c) A conclusion
questions.answer:
THE IMPORTANCE OF EDUCATION IN NATIONAL DEVELOPMENT

Introduction:
Education is universally recognised as the foundation upon which every successful nation is built. It is the process of acquiring knowledge, skills, values, and attitudes that equip individuals to contribute meaningfully to society. For a developing nation like Nigeria, education is not merely a privilege but an absolute necessity for achieving sustainable growth and development.

Body Paragraph 1 — Human Capital Development:
The most direct contribution of education to national development is the production of skilled human capital. A nation's greatest resource is its people, and education transforms raw human potential into productive capacity. Doctors, engineers, teachers, lawyers, and entrepreneurs — the professionals who drive economic activity — are all products of the educational system. Countries that invest heavily in education, such as South Korea and Singapore, have transformed from developing nations into economic powerhouses within a generation, demonstrating the extraordinary power of human capital development.

Body Paragraph 2 — Reduction of Poverty and Inequality:
Education is one of the most powerful tools for breaking the cycle of poverty. Educated individuals are more likely to secure employment, earn higher incomes, and make better decisions about health, family planning, and financial management. When education is made accessible to all — including girls, rural communities, and the economically disadvantaged — it reduces inequality and distributes the benefits of national growth more broadly. Research consistently shows that each additional year of schooling increases an individual's earning potential significantly.

Body Paragraph 3 — Promoting Good Governance and Civic Participation:
An educated citizenry is essential for a functioning democracy. Education produces citizens who can read, analyse information critically, and make informed decisions at the ballot box. It creates awareness of rights and responsibilities, reduces susceptibility to political manipulation, and nurtures the values of accountability, transparency, and rule of law. Furthermore, educated leaders are better equipped to formulate sound policies, manage public resources wisely, and engage in evidence-based decision-making.

Body Paragraph 4 — Technological and Scientific Advancement:
In the 21st century, national development is increasingly driven by technological innovation. Education — particularly in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) — equips a nation to participate in and contribute to the global knowledge economy. Without investment in quality education, nations remain consumers of technology produced elsewhere rather than creators of their own solutions to local challenges.

Conclusion:
In conclusion, education is the single most important investment any nation can make in its future. Its benefits extend far beyond the individual to touch every aspect of national life — economic, social, political, and technological. Nigeria and other developing nations must therefore prioritise quality, accessible, and relevant education if they are to achieve their full development potential. As Nelson Mandela wisely noted, education is the most powerful weapon we can use to change the world.

questions.question:
Answer the following comprehension questions based on the passage:

Passage:
The phenomenon of brain drain — the emigration of highly trained and educated individuals from developing to developed countries — has become one of the most serious challenges facing Africa today. Nigeria, with its vast population and significant pool of talented professionals, is among the countries most severely affected. Every year, thousands of doctors, nurses, engineers, academics, and other skilled workers leave Nigeria to seek better opportunities in Europe, North America, and other developed regions.

The causes of this exodus are not difficult to identify. Poor remuneration remains the primary driver — a Nigerian doctor may earn in a month what a counterpart in the United Kingdom earns in a day. Compounding this is the deterioration of infrastructure and working conditions. Hospitals lack basic equipment; universities are poorly funded; power supply is unreliable; and roads remain impassable. For many professionals, the decision to leave is not driven by a lack of patriotism but by the desire for an environment where their skills can be properly utilised and rewarded.

The consequences for Nigeria are devastating. The healthcare system, already overstretched, loses trained personnel faster than institutions can replace them. Universities struggle to retain experienced lecturers. The technology sector, despite enormous potential, cannot attract and keep the talent it needs. Ultimately, brain drain creates a vicious cycle: as skilled workers leave, public services deteriorate further, prompting more people to emigrate.

However, the situation is not entirely hopeless. Several African nations have begun implementing policies to reverse this trend. Competitive salaries, improved working conditions, diaspora engagement programmes, and the creation of technology hubs and innovation centres have shown promise. Nigeria must learn from these examples and create an environment where talent is valued, rewarded, and given the tools to thrive.

Questions:
(a) What is brain drain as explained in the passage? (2 marks)
(b) State TWO causes of brain drain mentioned in the passage. (2 marks)
(c) Explain in your own words what the writer means by 'a vicious cycle'. (2 marks)
(d) What THREE solutions are suggested in the passage? (3 marks)
(e) For each of these words as used in the passage, give another word or phrase that has the same meaning: (i) emigration (ii) exodus (iii) remuneration (iv) deterioration (3 marks)
questions.answer:
(a) Brain drain as explained in the passage:
Brain drain refers to the emigration (movement away) of highly trained and educated professionals from developing countries like Nigeria to developed countries such as those in Europe and North America, where they seek better opportunities.

(b) Two causes of brain drain:
1. Poor remuneration — Nigerian professionals are paid far less than their counterparts in developed countries (e.g. a Nigerian doctor earns in a month what a UK doctor earns in a day).
2. Deterioration of infrastructure and working conditions — hospitals lack equipment, universities are poorly funded, electricity supply is unreliable, and roads are in poor condition.

(c) 'A vicious cycle' as used in the passage:
The writer means a self-reinforcing negative situation where each problem makes the others worse. As skilled workers leave, public services become worse; and because public services are worse, more skilled workers decide to leave — creating a continuous downward spiral with no easy escape.

(d) Three solutions suggested in the passage:
1. Paying competitive salaries to skilled workers.
2. Improving working conditions in key sectors.
3. Creating diaspora engagement programmes to attract Nigerians abroad back home.
4. Establishing technology hubs and innovation centres to attract and retain talent.

(e) Vocabulary:
(i) Emigration — movement out of a country; departure abroad; relocation to another country.
(ii) Exodus — mass departure; large-scale movement of people out of a place.
(iii) Remuneration — payment; salary; wages; compensation for work done.
(iv) Deterioration — worsening; decline; degradation; decay.

questions.question:
Study the following and answer the questions on Summary:

Passage (for summary):
In recent years, social media has transformed the way young Nigerians communicate, access information, and engage with the world around them. Platforms such as Twitter (now X), Instagram, TikTok, and WhatsApp have become central to daily life for millions of Nigerian youth, offering unprecedented opportunities for connection, creativity, and commerce. Yet for all its benefits, social media also poses serious risks that demand careful consideration.

On the positive side, social media has democratised information. News, educational content, and professional knowledge that were once accessible only to the privileged few are now available to anyone with a smartphone and an internet connection. Young entrepreneurs have built thriving businesses through Instagram and WhatsApp, reaching customers they could never have found through traditional means. Activists and civic organisations have used Twitter to mobilise citizens, hold government accountable, and draw international attention to injustices — the #EndSARS protest of 2020 being the most vivid Nigerian example.

Nevertheless, the negative dimensions of social media are equally significant. The spread of misinformation and fake news is perhaps the gravest concern. False information travels faster and further than the truth on social platforms, with potentially dangerous consequences — from ethnic tension inflamed by fabricated stories to health decisions based on medical misinformation. Furthermore, excessive social media use has been linked to declining mental health among young people, with studies showing connections between heavy usage and anxiety, depression, and poor self-esteem, particularly when young people compare themselves unfavourably to the carefully curated lives of others online.

The challenge, therefore, is not to abandon social media — an unrealistic proposition in the digital age — but to use it wisely and responsibly. Media literacy education must be integrated into school curricula to equip young Nigerians with the skills to evaluate information critically. Parents and guardians also have a role to play in monitoring usage and encouraging healthy digital habits.

Question:
In not more than 100 words, summarise the ADVANTAGES of social media as presented in the passage.
questions.answer:
SUMMARY — Advantages of Social Media:

Social media has brought significant benefits to Nigerian youth. It has democratised access to information, making news, education, and professional knowledge available to all smartphone users regardless of social background. Young entrepreneurs have used platforms like Instagram and WhatsApp to build successful businesses and reach wider customer bases. Additionally, social media has become a powerful tool for civic engagement — enabling activists and organisations to mobilise citizens, demand government accountability, and amplify calls for justice, as demonstrated powerfully by the #EndSARS protest of 2020, which drew both national and international attention to police brutality in Nigeria.

[Word count: approximately 95 words]

questions.question:
Write a speech to be delivered at your school's inter-house sports competition on the topic: 'The Role of Sports in Building a Healthy and United Nation'.

Your speech should include: proper address, introduction, at least THREE main points, and a conclusion.
questions.answer:
The Role of Sports in Building a Healthy and United Nation

The Principal, Vice Principal, teachers, fellow students, distinguished guests, and sports enthusiasts — good morning and welcome to this wonderful occasion.

I am truly honoured to stand before you today to speak on a subject that lies very close to our hearts, especially on a day like this: the role of sports in building a healthy and united nation.

First and foremost, sports is one of the most powerful tools for promoting physical health. In an era where non-communicable diseases such as obesity, diabetes, and hypertension are rising at alarming rates — including among young people — regular physical activity through sports is not a luxury but a necessity. When young Nigerians run on a track, swim in a pool, or compete on a football field, they are investing in their long-term health. A healthy population is a productive population, and a productive population is the foundation of national development.

Secondly, sports has an extraordinary power to unite people across ethnic, religious, and social divides. Nigeria is a nation of over 250 ethnic groups and two major religions — a diversity that is our greatest strength but can also be a source of tension. Yet when the Super Eagles take the field, we forget those divisions entirely. We are not Yoruba, Igbo, or Hausa in that moment — we are Nigerians. Sports creates a shared identity, a common language, and a collective purpose that politics and religion often fail to achieve.

Thirdly, sports builds character and instils values that are essential for nation-building. Discipline, perseverance, teamwork, respect for rules, and the ability to accept both victory and defeat with grace — these are the qualities that great athletes develop on the field and carry into their careers, families, and civic lives. A nation of disciplined, team-oriented citizens is a nation that can solve its problems and achieve its goals.

Finally, sports creates economic opportunities. Nigerian athletes competing on the global stage attract foreign investment, tourism, and international goodwill. Sports industries — including equipment manufacturing, media broadcasting, coaching, and events management — generate employment and contribute to GDP.

In conclusion, sports is far more than entertainment or competition. It is a vehicle for health, unity, character development, and economic growth. Let us therefore commit today — as students, as teachers, as parents, and as citizens — to nurturing a culture of sports at every level of our national life.

Thank you, and let the games begin!
